coredata.dc:title: Ag@C decorated GaN nanoflower enabled super-stable, single molecule level SERS substrate integrated with machine learning for multiple analytes identification
coredata.dc:creator: He, Q.
Qiu, J.
Han, Y.
Wang, M.
Zhang, Y.
Han, L.
coredata.dc:description: Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) is the most promising noninvasive detection method. However, the long-term stability and multiple analytes identification are still challenging for the SERS sensor, especially for Ag materials, and inferior spectral interpretation in multispectral. Here, we developed a super-stable, single molecule-level SERS sensor via one-step synthesizing Ag@ organic carbon nanodots nanoparticles on the nanoflower gallium nitride surface. The SERS sensor not only achieves single molecule detection with the enhancement factor of 2.483 × 109 but also provides excellent long-term stability in air up to 8 months. The super-stability mechanism of the SERS substrate was proposed and discussed. In addition, integrating with machine learning technology-the back-propagation neural algorithm networks classifiers, three similar analytes were correctly recognized with an accuracy rate of 92.64%. This work could explore to the practical applications in the fields of the environmental pollution, food safety, and biomedicine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface-enhanced Raman scattering
Ag@C NPs synthesis
Single molecule detection
Long-term stability
Multianalytes analysis
Neural networks

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design and characterizations of SERS sensor
Single molecule detection via the SERS platform
The uniformity, long-term stability, and mechanism
Multiple toxins classification by machine learning

Shandong Province Department of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62174101), the Major Scientific and Technological Innovation Project of Shandong Province (2021CXGC010603), the Fundamental Research Funds of Shandong University (2020QNQT001). The authors would like to thank Collaborative Innovation Center of Technology and Equipment for Biological Diagnosis and Therapy in Universities of Shandong, and Haiyan Yu, Xiaomin Zhao, Sen Wang and Yuyu Guo from State Key Laboratory of Microbial Technology of Shandong University for help and guidance in material characterization.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62174101 ), the Major Scientific and Technological Innovation Project of Shandong Province ( 2021CXGC010603 ), the Fundamental Research Funds of Shandong University ( 2020QNQT001 ). The authors would like to thank Collaborative Innovation Center of Technology and Equipment for Biological Diagnosis and Therapy in Universities of Shandong, and Haiyan Yu, Xiaomin Zhao, Sen Wang and Yuyu Guo from State Key Laboratory of Microbial Technology of Shandong University for help and guidance in material characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic diagram of the experimental design. (a) The fabrication process of Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN SERS substrate. (b) The structure diagram of back-propagation neural network for decomposition of mixed spectrum. Abbreviations: SERS, surface-enhanced Raman scattering; GaN, gallium nitride; NFs, nanoflowers.

Material characterization during substrate preparation. (a) The absorption spectrum of AgNPs (blue curve), organic carbon nanodots (red curve), and Ag@C NPs (black curve). The TEM image (b) and size distribution (c) of the Ag@C NPs at synthesizing time of 12 h. (d) The SEM of NFs GaN wafer. The SEM image (e) and size distribution (f) of Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN SERS substrate synthesized via one-step synthesis method at 12 h. The EDX of the NFs GaN (g) and Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN (h) SERS substrate, insets are the elements mapping. (i) The SERS enhanced performance and contact angles of the Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN SERS substrates with different synthesizing time (0 h, 2 h, 5 h, 8 h, 10 h, 12 h, 14 h, 16 h, 18 h, 24 h). Abbreviations: SERS, surface-enhanced Raman scattering; GaN, gallium nitride; NFs, nanoflowers; EDX, energy dispersive X-ray.

SERS detection of R6G. (a) Comparison of enhancement effects of different substrates including NFs GaN (black curve), Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN (green curve), AgNPs/NFs GaN (red curve), and Ag@C NPs/planar GaN (blue curve). (b) The hydrophobicity of the above four substrates. (c) SERS detection results of R6G on the Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN SERS substrate and divided into two domains including linear detection and single molecule statistical domains. (d) Raman spectra of R6G with different concentrations in linear detection domain. (e) The linear relationship between the logarithm of analyte concentration and logarithm of intensity at 613 cm−1 in linear detection domain. (f) The linear relationship between the logarithm of analyte concentration and probability of effective signal appearance in the single molecule statistical domain. Abbreviations: SERS, surface-enhanced Raman scattering; GaN, gallium nitride; NFs, nanoflowers; R6G, rhodamine 6G.

Probing the uniformity and stability of the Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN SERS substrate. (a) Raman mapping of the intensity at 613 cm−1 peak of R6G (1 μL). (b) SERS spectra obtained on Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN SERS substrate at different time nodes. (c) The normalized comparison of Raman intensities at 613 cm−1 peak of R6G over time (1 μL) on the Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN and AgNPs/NFs GaN SERS substrate. (d) The energy level relationship of AgNPs with oxide layer and carbon dot insulation layer clearly illuminate activated state of stability. Abbreviations: SERS, surface-enhanced Raman scattering; GaN, gallium nitride; NFs, nanoflowers; R6G, rhodamine 6G.

Machine learning model to predictive decomposition of mixed spectra. (a) The structural framework of back-propagation neural network, from the input layer to the hidden layer, and finally through the activation function to output the result. (b) SERS spectra of combination mixture of three seawater prohibited drugs as the input of neural network. (c) The hot mapping of input matrix which the parameter extracted from the preprocessed SERS spectra. (d) The predict classification result mapping via the back-propagation neural network. (e) Predicted results of test dataset for the toxin mixture. (f) The confusion matrix of test result after neural network training. Abbreviations: SERS, surface-enhanced Raman scattering; GaN, gallium nitride; NFs, nanoflowers; R6G, rhodamine 6G.

head.ce:title: Ag@C decorated GaN nanoflower enabled super-stable, single molecule level SERS substrate integrated with machine learning for multiple analytes identification

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Marine Science and Technology, Shandong University, Qingdao, Shandong, 266237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Marine Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Marine Science and Technology, Shandong University, Qingdao, Shandong, 266237, China

ce:footnote.ce:label: a
ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Qihang He and Jiaoyan Qiu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) is the most promising noninvasive detection method. However, the long-term stability and multiple analytes identification are still challenging for the SERS sensor, especially for Ag materials, and inferior spectral interpretation in multispectral. Here, we developed a super-stable, single molecule-level SERS sensor via one-step synthesizing Ag@ organic carbon nanodots nanoparticles on the nanoflower gallium nitride surface. The SERS sensor not only achieves single molecule detection with the enhancement factor of 2.483 × 109 but also provides excellent long-term stability in air up to 8 months. The super-stability mechanism of the SERS substrate was proposed and discussed. In addition, integrating with machine learning technology-the back-propagation neural algorithm networks classifiers, three similar analytes were correctly recognized with an accuracy rate of 92.64%. This work could explore to the practical applications in the fields of the environmental pollution, food safety, and biomedicine.

In this work, we developed a super stable, single molecule level SERS sensor via one-step synthesizing Ag@ organic carbon nanodots nanoparticles on the nano-flower Gallium Nitride surface. The SERS sensor not only achieves single molecule detection with the enhancement factor of 2.483 × 109, but also provides excellent long-term stability in air up to eight months. The super-stability mechanism of the SERS substrate was proposed and discussed. In addition, integrating with machine learning technology-the back-propagation neural algorithm networks classifiers, three similar analytes were correctly recognized with an accuracy rate of 92.64%. This work could explore to the practical applications in the fields of the environmental pollution, food safety and biomedicine.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since 1974, Fleischmann reported that the phenomenon of Raman signals unexpected enhancement from pyridine molecule adsorbed on rough silver surface, the surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) spectroscopy had gradually become a significant analytical method for its non-destructive, high sensitivity, fingerprint characteristic, and label-free detection [1,2]. Moreover, SERS technology realizes high resolution with a little and no sample prefabrication, even reaching the single-molecule level [3,4]. Therefore, SERS has been extensively applied in many filed, such as public security investigation, clinical diagnosis, environment monitoring, chemical synthesis, and food safety [5–7]. Indeed, the core of Raman enhancement technology is an intense electromagnetic field of substrate material, so-called ‘hot spots’, which provides local surface plasma resonance (LSPR) [8,9]. However, the conditions for the hot spots with sufficient density on the substrate surface are harsh, which are closely related to the material properties, surface nanomorphology of substrate as well as the excitation light [10,11]. In recent years, large numbers of micro-nano processing technologies have been explored for the preparation of high enhanced and ultrasensitive SERS substrates. For example, Zhou's group designed 3D Au nanowire array, which fabricated by sequential nano-transfer printing, for Alzheimer's disease detection [12]. To improve the imaging efficiency of in vivo Raman spectroscopy, Shan et al. synthesized Au@Au–Ag dot-in-cubic nano-frames based on galvanic replacement reaction and atom codeposition technologies for the detection of micro-tumors cell [13]. Although the above methods have been able to prepare high enhancement substrates, it is still a challenge to programmable manufacture SERS substrate with large-scale high sensitivity, stability, reproducibility, and homogeneity via a simple one-step synthesis method, especially based on Ag [14].

Compared with other noble metals such as Au and Pt, Ag nanomaterial have been considered as the most promising SERS substrate for its more active plasmon effect, stronger inherent electromagnetic enhancement efficiency, and lower cost [15,16]. Nevertheless, the development and application of substrate based on AgNPs are still limited because its oxidation susceptibility to air and water causes the transformation of LSPR mode and destruction of SERS activity [16,17]. In the recent, numbers of researchers are trying to find out various methods to improve its stability with maintaining the strong enhancement characteristics. For example, Yuan and coworkers reported that the Ag@Au alloy method can successfully combine the sensitivity of Ag and the stability of Au [18]. Li et al. reported that coating Ag with an inert oxide layer (SiO2 and Al2O3) can passivate the surface oxidation [19]. Despite the maintainability of stability, these methods still are suffering from uncontrollable proportional adjustment and complex technological process. Thus, the long-term stability is often difficult to achieve in a highly enhanced, reproducible, and large-area tunable fashion, which directly makes it difficult for Ag-based SERS in practical applications.

Organic carbon nanodots, as one of nano sized carbon materials with size less than 10 nm, have triggered more attention because of their photo-induced electron transfer property, high photostability, and specific surface area compared to inert metal material [20–23]. The abundant surface functional grouping and electronic defect of carbon nanodots promote them can serve as an excellent electron donor and electron acceptor [24,25]. In addition, the composition of carbon nanodots with noble metal nanoparticles owning unique LSPR properties can change the single structure of the respective components.The formed interface can also promote the separation transfer of charges [26,27]. Owing to the unique electrical properties of carbon nanodots, they can be utilized as a protective layer for Ag material without loss of LSPR.

The detection of single substance in ideal system was reported fruitily. Unfortunately, the natural environment is more complex, diverse, and heterogeneous system, which promoted the difficulty to detect the targets accurately [28]. The SERS can provide characteristic fingerprint spectra of the substances, it is still a challenge to distinguish species between similar species [29]. In order to overcome the identification problem of detection results in a complex actual environment, machine learning such as artificial neural networks (ANN) has been introduced into SERS spectroscopy technology [30]. The supervised ANN has been extensively applied in fields such as medical diagnosis, image identification and machine vision, and it benefits from the following main advantages [31]. First, the deep middle layer provides a multilevel optimization platform for feature extraction. Second, the non-linear mapping relationship achieves more complex and more relevant species classification requirements than the linear relationship. Lastly, the ANN based on the supervised learning model have strong generalization capability, which the trained networks can be well applied to the recognition of new patterns [32–34]. Nevertheless, typical ANN shows poor accuracy and reliability of networks when it is lacking the initial training database. SERS mapping can provide large amounts of raw spectra data for networks training at short notice or even a few seconds [35]. So, it has become a new promising development direction to construct a flexible discrimination tool combining machine learning algorithms with SERS spectroscopy [36].

In this work, based on previous studies on gallium nitride (GaN) SERS substrates [37,38], we developed a highly sensitive and long-term stable hydrophobic SERS substrate via one-step synthesizing hydrophobic AgNPs on the nanoflowers like surface of gallium nitride wafer combining with the machine learning for discriminatory detection of the multiple antibacterial toxins. This hydrophobic SERS substrate was constructed by hybriding the AgNPs with organic carbon nanodots, which prevents water molecules and oxygen to deliver excellent long-term stability with single molecular detection performance. In addition, the statistical detection performance of the SERS sensor in the case of infinitesimal analyte was evaluated based on mapping scanning technology. Finally, a decision classifier based on the SERS spectrum combined with ANN was built for multispecies classification in complex water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and characterizations of SERS sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 depicts the typical fabrication and application principle of hydrophobic nanoflowers SERS substrate using a series of steps, including nanoflowers gallium nitride (NFs GaN) grown by metal organics chemical vapor deposition (MOCVD), one-step hydration method of carbon composite silver nanoparticles (Ag@C NPs) on GaN wafer, and SERS spectral interpretation applications based on back-propagation (BP) neural networks. First, the Ag@C NPs could be synthesized the one-step synthesis in aqueous solution method without GaN wafer. To the successful synthesis of Ag@C NPs, the UV–vis spectra of AgNPs, organic carbon nanodots and Ag@C NPs were measured (Fig. 2 a). The Ag@C NPs had two obvious absorption peaks which were absorption peaks of silver nanoparticles at ∼400 nm and carbon dots at 255 nm. In addition, the absorption peak of silver had bathochromic shift, from 405 nm of AgNPs to 420 nm of Ag@C NPs, indicating that introduction of organic carbon nanodot changed the morphology of AgNPs and resonant excitation mode of plasmon [39]. To further evaluate the surface topography of nanoparticles, the transmission electron microscope (TEM) was on the Ag@C NPs as shown in Fig. 2b. To further confirm the morphological structure of Ag/C, we conducted TEM EDS images of Ag/C. As shown in Fig. S1. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) results showed the clear content of Ag and C and Ag/C nanocomposites. The synthesized Ag@C NPs discretely distributed in the organic solvent ethanol and spaced out ∼1 nm for charge repulsion, and an average diameter of the Ag@C NPs is typically about 15 nm in Fig. 2c. Next, the NFs GaN wafer was grown by MOCVD, and the scanning electron microscope (SEM) image (Fig. 2d) clearly showed that nano clusters with smooth petals randomly distributed on the surface. The hydrophobic Ag@C NPs could be densely distributed on the surface of the nanoflowers through the one-step hydration method for 12 h as shown in Fig. 2e and f exhibited that the average particle size of the hydrophobic nanoparticles is also ∼15 nm, which is consistent with the individually synthesized nanoparticles. To further examine the hydrophobic nanoparticles on the surface of NFs GaN, the energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) mappings were measured on the SERS substrate as shown in Fig. 2g and h. In addition to the Ga, N, and C elements contained in NFs GaN substrate, Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN also contained Ag element, and the markedly increased content of C element, indicating successful growth of Ag@C NPs on the NFs GaN substrate surface. During the one-step growth, the synthesis time affected the size of Ag@C NPs, which in turn affected the LSPR, so the optimization of time condition was essential. As shown in Fig. 2i, the enhancement effect of the SERS substrate gradually became stronger with time, reaching to the maximum at 12 h, and then turning to a declining trend. It can be explained by the Ag@C NPs distribution density on the NFs GaN. As shown in Fig. S2, the denser Ag@C NPs distribution upon increased growth time, and excessive growth time will lead to the Ag@C NPs aggregation film resulting in a weak electromagnetic field. In addition, the surface property of the substrate turns to hydrophobic from the hydrophilic gradually with the Ag@C NPs growth on the NFs GaN substrate. The hydrophobic angle could reach 125° at the 12 h growth conditions, which may promote the SERS sensitivity for analytes detection through analytes enrichment on the SERS substrate [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Single molecule detection via the SERS platform
ce:section.ce:para: The rhodamine 6G (R6G) molecule as a typical Raman analyte was selected to investigate and compare the performance of the different SERS substrates as shown in Fig. 3 a. The NFs GaN hardly enhanced the R6G signal, and only a faint peak can be observed at 417 cm−1 (mark with ∗ after magnifying 25 times in Fig. 3a), indicating that the fundamental source of the enhancement effect was not the bare NFs GaN. The strongest R6G Raman signal spectrum was observed with Ag nanomaterials modification on NFs GaN, which was contributed to LPSR of the AgNPs. The R6G Raman signal was slightly weakening on the Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN substrate due to the carbon dots incorporation, but it's still very strong. Further weakened R6G Raman signal on the Ag@C NPs/planar GaN substrate indicates that the NFs GaN structures gave the contribution of the SERS intensity due to the rougher surface and the larger specific surface area. It's well known that the Ag has strong electromagnetic field SERS enhancement and carbon has the chemical SERS enhancement. Therefore, SERS enhancement of the Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN SERS substrate would come from the synergistic enhancement effect between the electromagnetic enhancement of Ag and chemical enhancement of C. Fig. 3b shows the water contact angles of the different substrates. Due to the hydrophilicity of AgNPs, the contact angle of the AgNPs/NFs GaN substrate was less than bared NFs GaN (Fig. 3b). The NFs and planar GaN substrates modificated with Ag@C NPs showed hydrophobic property with contact angles of 125 and 114°, respectively, which promoted the stability of the substrates.

To evaluate the detection performance of the Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN SERS substrate, different concentrations from 1 pM to 1 μM of R6G solutions were dropped onto the surface for detection by the mapping scanning technology. As exhibited in Fig. 3c, the systematic detection results were divided into two domain including linear detection and single molecule statistical. Here, it was defined that the peak intensity of R6G at 613 cm−1 was higher than that of the background as the effective detection signal. When the effective signal occupancy rate was 100%, it was a linear detection domain in which the average effective signal intensity of mapping changed drastically with the concentrations of the analyte. Otherwise, it belongs to the single molecular statistics domain, and a linear relationship was obtained in this domain by counting the effective signal appearance probability of the mapping scan area (in Figs. S3–S8). Within the linear detection domain, the remarkable Raman spectra were obtained at 613 cm−1 of R6G with concentrated from 10 nM to 1 μM (Fig. 3d). In Fig. 3e, there was a linear relationship between the logarithm of the Raman signal intensity (lg[intensity]) and the logarithm value of the corresponding concentration (lg[CR6G]) (lg[intensity] = 0.8868 × lg[CR6G] + 9.816) with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9937. In the single molecule statistics domain, the probability of effective signal was proportional to the logarithm of R6G concentration in the range from 1 pM to 1 nM. As shown in Fig. 3f, the fitting relationship was y = 30.1933 × lg[CR6G] + 366.667, in which the ordinate represented the probability of effective signal. However, as the concentration further decreases to less than 1 pM, no signal was observed no matter how many repeated detections (Fig. S8), implying that the detection limit of the Ag@C NPs/NFs SERS substrate had been reached to 1 pM. Finally, the enhancement factor (EF) of Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN SERS substrate was calculated to be ∼2.483 × 109 (detailed calculation formulas were demonstrated in Supporting information, and correlative data were displayed in Fig. S9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The uniformity, long-term stability, and mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: As known to all, although Ag had more active surface plasma properties than Au, the application of SERS substrate based on AgNPs was limited in its poor uniformity and long-term stability. To assess the uniformity of the Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN SERS substrate, the SERS mapping (75 μm × 75 μm) of R6G was measured within the linear detection domain (1 nM). Fig. 4 a showed the mapping image with almost similar color gamut distribution. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of 12.46% was obtained, demonstrating that the Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN SERS substrate had a good uniformity without the influence of the nanoflowers roughness. To verify the long-term stability of the SERS substrates with different silver-based materials, the SERS signals of R6G were collected every week with the SERS substrate storage at room temperature in the laboratory. As shown in Fig. 4b, the SERS intensity of R6G is almost as same as the as-preparation after 32 weeks. However, it drops to half of the initial intensity in the first week for the AgNPs/NFs GaN substrate (Fig. S10). Fig. 4c gives the time-dependent SERS intensity of R6G at 613 cm−1. Compared of the signal reproducibility of AgNPs/NFs GaN substrate, the R6G Raman intensity on the Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN substrate was basically stable at around 91.3% with a RSD of 4.18%, proving that the Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN SERS has super-stability in air. While, the R6G Raman intensity on the Ag NPs/NFs GaN substrate decayed exponentially, which means that the Ag NPs were oxidized quickly in air. At present, many researches focus on improving the stability of SERS substrate from the aspects of process and materials as summarized in Table S1. Among them, the Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN SERS substrate delivered the best long-term stability. Here, we attributed the super-stability characteristic of the Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN SERS substrate to the insulating protective effect of organic carbon nanodots for hindering the inherent oxidation of Ag. The possible mechanism of super-stability was depicted in Fig. 4d. In oxygen water environment, the bared AgNPs was oxidized to generate dense oxide layer Ag2O driven by the oxygen molecules and hydrogen proton, which adsorbed on the surface of AgNPs (4Ag + O2 + 4H+ → 4Ag+ + 2H2O). After the excitation light was incident, a large number of particles were pumped to the upper energy level, generating plasmons on the surface of AgNPs. Then, dense Ag2O layer had a strong damping effect and quickly transferred the hot electrons on the surface; therefore, the molecule signal enhancement of the substrate weakened rapidly. In contrast to bare Ag NPs, hydrophobic effect of Ag@C NPs reduced the specific surface area contacted by water molecules in the air, and Ag was better isolated from the oxygen under the protection of organic carbon nanodots. Therefore, the optical response of Ag@C NPs still exhibited a main LSPR, although a negligible part of which was converted into hot electron and transferred to the carbon dots, causing the effective Raman signal to decrease. These experiments and mechanisms indicated that excellent long-term stability of Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN SERS substrate could extend the service lifetime for actual industrial detection and storage. In practical applications, it is often necessary to face different water bodies and interference. We investigated the detection effect of the sensor under different pH values (5, 7, 8) and different water bodies (tap water (pH = 5), DI water (pH = 7), and sea water (pH = 8)). Fig. S11 (a, c, e) showed the SERS spectra of CV with different concentrations (10−7 M, 10−6 M and 10−5 M) corresponding the RSD of SERS intensity (Fig. S11(b, d, f)) of CV at the peak of 1620 cm−1. The results showed slight variation with RSD of 3.95%–9.82%, indicating the good stability of the SERS substrate in acid and basic water bodies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multiple toxins classification by machine learning
ce:section.ce:para: In real biological samples, drug toxins often existed in the form of a mixture of multiple toxins rather than a single species. To provide toxin classification capabilities of SERS spectra detection, BP neural networks were processed on mixed spectrum. As exhibited in Fig. 5 a, after the mixed spectra were input, the main toxin components in the mixed spectrum were predicted via the BP neural network. Here, two triphenylmethane antibacterial agents, crystal violet (CV) and malachite green (MG), as well as a phenothiazine antibacterial agents, methylene blue (MB), were used as classical representatives to validate the ability of this system to the interpretation and classify. Multiplex SERS spectra of three substances and their mixture were collected in Fig. 5b, in which including MB and CV molecules was numerous overlaps and hardly distinguishable. Hence, we segmented those 7 × 65 spectra and extracted the number of peak and position of the strongest peak in each segment as the characteristic value to enhance the contrast of input training database (Fig. 5c).

In the supervised learning mode, we defined the output vector through binary coding technology to characterize the components contained in the multi-spectrum of putout result, in which 1 indicates the presence of ingredient content and 0 means absence. In order to improve the training speed, an algorithm with momentum adaptive learning rate (MALR) factor in the training phase of BP network was proposed. In the case of a constant learning rate, the momentum factor was adaptively adjusted according to the gradient change of the error function with respect to the weight vector. The main program of neural networks on the Matlab is provided in Supporting materials. After the network trained on the test database, the prediction classification of the actual multiplex spectra was shown in Fig. 5d. After rounding to the nearest, the detailed predicted results and confusion matrix of test datasets are shown in Fig. 5e and f. The results showed the single component was well identified with 91.42% accuracy, and mixture composition also was well classified with 82.85% accuracy. The proposed machine learning-based SERS detection strategy could reach the final prediction classification accuracy of 92.64% for prohibited drug multispectral. These results demonstrate that the BP neural network-based SERS strategy was broadly generalizable to predicted species classification. It is essential for most practical applications, for example, in cell tumor analysis, where tumor type may be determined via SERS spectrum of tissue section, or in chemical synthesis, where intermediate products may be identified in a minute case.

In conclusion, we have prepared a novel ultra-stable SERS platform for quantitative multiple analytes analysis through one-step assembling hydrophobic Ag@C NPs on the surface of NFs GaN in aqueous solution. Based on the effective protection of organic carbon dots for silver, the platform can achieve stability for up to eight months without losing the enhancement effect, which enhancement factor was 2.483 × 109. Moreover, the platform can achieve single-molecule-level detection of R6G molecules combined with statistical analysis, with limit of detection of 1 pM. In addition, combined with the mapping spectrum detection of SERS and the momentum adaptive neural network algorithm, a technical method for simultaneous detection and analysis of multiple substances was constructed, and the accurate classification of antibiotics was achieved, with an accuracy rate of 92.64%. Therefore, Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN SERS platform would provide a novel pathway for solving the long-term stability of SERS substrates and promote the development of environmental science and biological detection field.

Materials and Apparatus: Silver nitrate (AgNO3, ≥99.9%), cetylpyridinium chloride monohydrate (CPC, ≥99.0%), and rhodamine 6G (R6G) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥99%) was purchased from Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Crystal violet (CV), methylene blue (MB), malachite Green (MG), and (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES, ≥99%) were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ), which purified using a Millipore filtration system, was used in all procedures.

Fabrication of NFs GaN substrate: 4 inch NFs GaN were synthesized on c-plane aluminum oxide (Al2O3) substrate by metal organic chemical vapor deposition (MOCVD) system. Firstly, low temperature GaN buffer layer was grown at 550 °C with annealing at 1040 °C for 120 s by introducing NH3 and trimethylgallium (TMGa) at a pressure of 100 mbar in H2 followed by 2 μm unintentionally doped GaN layer at 1020 °C. Then, 3 nm InxGa1 − xN (x = 0.15–0.25) with Si-doping concentration of 5 × 1018/cm3 was grown in N2 ambient at low pressure. Finally, the NFs GaN with Si-doping concentration of 1 × 1020/cm3 was grown at 1020 °C for 600 s by introducing NH3 and TMGa at a pressure of 100 mbar in N2 ambient on top of InxGa1 − xN (x = 0.15–0.25).

In-situ synthesis of Ag@C/NFs GaN: The hydrophobicity Ag@C nanoparticles were synthesized according to the one-step synthesis in aqueous solution method with modifications to assemble on the NFs GaN substrate surface [41]. Briefly, the prepared NFs GaN were pretreated by plasma in oxygen atmosphere for 10 min to form the hydroxyl, then added 5% (v/v) APTES solution (diluted with ethanol solution) to statically modify at room temperature 8–12 h and purified with ultrapure water for 5–10 min. Then, 5 mL of 45 mM AgNO3 and 5 mL of 15 mM CPC solutions were added to the shading bottle and stirred 30 min, the color of the mixture changed milky white. Finally, immersed as-prepared surface modified NFs GaN in above mixture solution, and added the 2 mL of 1 M NaOH solution, the color immediately changed to brown-black and gradually darkened over time. After 12 h of in-situ growth in dark, the NFs GaN wafers were taken out and ultrasonic cleaned with ultrapure water, thus the hydrophobicity Ag@C/NFs GaN SERS substrate could be obtained.

SERS detection via mapping scanning technology: First, the analytes (R6G, MB, MG, and CV) with different concentrations were prepared. Second, the SERS substrate was put into the different prepared solutions for 2 h. Third, the SERS substrate was taken out and blow dry with N2 for SERS measurement. SERS detection was measured on the Renishaw InVia Raman spectrometer equipped with a 532 nm wavelength and intensity 5 mW laser and a 50 × long objective lens. During the detection, we randomly selected a SERS mapping area of 20 μm × 20 μm on the surface of Ag@C NPs/NFs GaN substrate, set the detection points of mapping with an interval of 2 μm, and the exposure time of each point was 1 s.

Machine learning model: BP neural networks were used to species prediction of complex seawater prohibited drugs. The frameworks of BP networks were organized with an input layer, followed by three hidden layers, and an output layer, where interlayer contained the weight values and activation function connecting the neurons. The networks rely on the BP error to modify the network weight value, so that the predicted output is infinitely close to the actual output. First, raw Raman spectroscopy data were obtained from three seawater prohibited drug (CV, MB, and MG) including their mixed solution, in which 525 spectra were used as network training data and the remaining 175 spectra as test data. Collected raw data were preprocessed for denoise, baseline correction, and main eigenvalues extraction, and these eigenvalues as independent variables were input the BP neural network. In this process, to improve the local comparability of the spectra, each spectrum was divided into four segments along the wave number direction, and the main eigenvalues including the number of peaks and strongest peak position were obtained from each segments via the peak finding function. Notably, the positions of the strongest peak rather than intensities were eigenvalues because of fingerprint characteristics of the Raman spectrum, and avoiding noise and overinformation caused the difference of experimental laser intensity and concentration. Before the input vector was inputted, it needed to be normalized in the range from −1 to 1 by the following formula.
(1)
X =
2
(
x − min
)
max − min
− 1
where max and min (1) respectively mean the maximum and minimum values in the input vector array. In hidden layers, each layer contained 10 neurons (y 1, y 2, …, y 10), which can be expressed by the following equation:
(2)
y j
= f
(
∑ i
w
i j
x i
)
where w
ij means weight value which connected between the j-th neuron in hidden layer and the i-th neuron in input layer. Notably, the outputs of previous layer were the input of the next layer, thus forming a progressive network. Meanwhile,
f
in the equation was the activation function which is nonlinear transformation of data to improve the expressive ability of neural networks. In our network, activation function used the log-sigmoid function to normalize the output in rang of 0 ot 1.
(3)
f
(
n e t
)
=
1
1 +
e
− n e t

In the supervised learning mode of BP neural network, to examine the difference of output results for multiple targets, defined the output vector through binary coding technology. Consequently, the existence of corresponding components in the mixed sample was defined as 1, otherwise it was defined as 0. For example, CV, mixture of CV and MB, mixture of the three were, respectively, labeled as (1,0,0), (1,1,0), and (1,1,1). In order to evaluate the learning efficiency of the network, the errors (E) were defined as loss function which calculated the difference between the prediction and target via mean square error function, as follows:
(4)
E =
1 2
(
T − Y
)
2
=
∑ k
(
t k
−
y k
)
2
2
where
T
and Y represent the expected output and network training output.

MALR algorithm which was improved BP neural network was proposed to solve the problems of conventional BP network including local minimum interference, low learning efficiency, and slow convergence. In MALR BP neural networks, when the errors were iterated and reversely propagated to hidden layers, the corresponding weight w could be adjusted via the following formula:
(5)
Δ w
(
t + 1
)
= η Δ w
( t )
+ α
(
1 − η
)
∂ E
( t )
∂ w
( t )
where η was momentum coefficient and it value ranged from 0 to 1. This indicated that part of the weight adjustment vector value at time t was superimposed on time t + 1, to reduce the amount of correction each time which helped to reduce network oscillation. At the same time, the α was the learning rate which was a fixed constant in the standard BP algorithm, but it was difficult to determine in practical application. Because if α was too small, the training times of error would increase in the flat region, and if too large, the error would skip over maximize of the slight change in the sharp region, which all would reduce the learning speed and accuracy, so adaptively adjustable learning rate was a necessity. Adaptive learning rate algorithm adjusted α via a step relationship which after a round of adjustment, if the error (E) increases, then modified α to βα (β < 1), otherwise the error (E) decreases, then further modified α to γα (γ > 1). In our case of toxin detection interpretation, the momentum coefficient η was set as 0.85, and learning rate α was set as 0.01.

Qihang He: Investigation, Methodology, Writing original draft. Jiaoyan Qiu: Writing Revised Manuscript, Response the Reviewers, drawing, Measurement. Qihang He and Jiaoyan Qiu contributed equally to this work. Yingkuan Han: Investigation, Measurement, Formal analysis. Min Wang: Methodology. Lin Han: Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yu Zhang: Writing review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62174101), the Major Scientific and Technological Innovation Project of Shandong Province (2021CXGC010603), the Fundamental Research Funds of Shandong University (2020QNQT001). The authors would like to thank Collaborative Innovation Center of Technology and Equipment for Biological Diagnosis and Therapy in Universities of Shandong, and Haiyan Yu, Xiaomin Zhao, Sen Wang and Yuyu Guo from State Key Laboratory of Microbial Technology of Shandong University for help and guidance in material characterization.
